name: Avebury Circle
description: Avebury Circle, located in Wiltshire, England, is the largest stone circle in the world, predating Stonehenge and possessing a unique, awe-inspiring presence. Constructed in the Neolithic period, around 3000-2500 BC, it consists of a massive outer circle encompassing two smaller inner circles. Within the circle lies the village of Avebury itself, a fascinating juxtaposition of ancient monument and modern life. Its mythological importance stems from its perceived connection to ancient rituals, ancestor worship, and the cycles of nature. The stones themselves are believed to embody ancestral spirits or represent powerful forces of nature. Some theories suggest Avebury was a sacred meeting place, a site for communal gatherings, or even a symbolic representation of the cosmos. The sheer scale of the monument suggests a significant investment of time and resources, indicating its profound importance to the people who built it. Folklore surrounding Avebury speaks of druids performing ceremonies within the circle, though archaeological evidence for this is limited. However, the enduring sense of mystery and spiritual power associated with Avebury ensures its continued relevance in British mythology and modern pagan beliefs. The site remains a potent symbol of Britain's ancient past and a place where people connect with the earth's energy and the spirits of their ancestors. Its vastness and intricate design encourage contemplation and speculation about the beliefs and practices of the Neolithic people who created this monumental landscape.
subtitle: World's largest stone circle.

keyMyths.title: The Serpent's Path
keyMyths.description: The Avenue, a processional pathway leading to Avebury, is said to represent a serpent, linking the earth and sky. This pathway was used for ritual processions during significant astronomical events.

keyMyths.title: The Stones as Ancestors
keyMyths.description: Each stone is believed to embody the spirit of an ancestor or a powerful figure from the past. Touching the stones is thought to connect one with the wisdom and energy of these ancestors.

sources.author: Burl, Aubrey
sources.work: The Stone Circles of Britain, Ireland and Brittany
sources.description: Comprehensive overview of stone circles, including Avebury.
sources.text: Comprehensive overview of stone circles, including Avebury.

sources.description: Argues for a lunar-solar cycle embedded within Avebury's design.
